channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Cornyn’s Defeat Fuels Tensions With President Trump in Senate G.O.P.
item.description: Senators are angry President Trump turned on a respected former leader whom they consider a loyal Republican. Now Mr. Trump faces resistance from his own embittered ranks.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Ilana Panich-Linsman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Cornyn, Republican of Texas, noted in his concession to Ken Paxton on Tuesday that he still had time left in the Senate.

item.title: Paxton’s Texas Victory Opens a New Front in the Battle for the Senate
item.description: Many Democrats and some Republicans said the scandal-plagued Ken Paxton’s victory could turn Texas into a battleground state that will determine Senate control.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton defeated Senator John Cornyn in a runoff for the Republican nomination on Tuesday, setting up a marquee race against James Talarico, the Democrat.

item.title: A Blue Texas May Be More Than a Dream for Democrats
item.description: Ken Paxton’s victory for the Republican nomination and a big shift among Hispanic voters have put a Senate seat within reach.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Alabama Republicans Ask Supreme Court to Allow New Congressional Map in Redistricting Fight
item.description: Republican leaders in the state have asked the justices to clear the way for a congressional map that a lower court found discriminated against Black voters.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court justices are familiar with the longstanding fight over Alabama’s congressional district map.

item.title: Reflecting Pool Contract Has ‘Inflated’ Profit Margin, Government Analysis Finds
item.description: The firm that was given a no-bid contract to fix the troubled landmark is charging 20 percent. The typical profit margin is 6 percent to 12 percent, internal records show.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Atlantic Industrial Coatings, the Virginia-based contractor given a no-bid contract to repair the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, is being paid $13.1 million, which is seven times the amount that President Trump initially said the work would cost.

item.title: National Park Entrance Fees Are Funding Trump’s D.C. Projects
item.description: The administration is spending at least $67 million worth of fees paid by visitors to national parks on fixing D.C. fountains and the Reflecting Pool.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Andrea Fuller
item.media:credit: Brontë Wittpenn/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The National Park Service is spending millions in fees paid by visitors to parks across the country, like Yosemite National Park in California, to pay for restoration of nine ornamental fountains in Washington, D.C.

item.title: Israel Continues Its Escalation in Lebanon, Ordering Evacuations
item.description: The Israeli military issued sweeping evacuation orders for two southern cities, indicating that more airstrikes were imminent.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: Villages in southern Lebanon after strikes on Wednesday.

item.title: Residents Watch as Israeli War Planes Circle Over Southern Lebanon
item.description: Israel has intensified its deadly military campaign against Hezbollah, the Iran-backed militant group, in recent days, striking targets across Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: Looking out from a rooftop in Baysarieh as strikes target villages in southern Lebanon on Wednesday.

item.title: Global Supply Shortages Deepen as War Drags On, Risking Jobs and Growth
item.description: After three months, the fallout of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is spreading, with developing countries bearing the brunt of the shortfall.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis, Catie Edmondson, Eshe Nelson, Peter S. Goodman and Suhasini Raj
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Picture Alliance, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar before the war with Iran.

item.title: A Quiet Pacific Village Becomes China’s Security Testing Ground
item.description: When a remote Pacific village asked for help with rowdy youth, the Chinese police arrived with a surveillance system. Then came the backlash.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson, Berry Wang and Adam Ferguson
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Fishing on the shoreline in Honiara on the island of Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands.

item.title: Biden Sues Justice Dept. to Block Release of Tapes
item.description: The former president argued that the Justice Department has a responsibility to protect the privacy of conversations he had with a former ghostwriter.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Recordings of conversations former President Joseph R. Biden Jr. had with his ghostwriter came into the Justice Department’s possession in 2023.

item.title: ’60 Minutes’ Journalist Sharyn Alfonsi Loses Deal After Dispute With Bari Weiss
item.description: Sharyn Alfonsi, whose segment on a brutal Salvadoran prison was pulled abruptly in December, said that CBS News and its top editor, Bari Weiss, had let her contract expire.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Marla Aufmuth/Getty Images for Texas Conference for Women
item.media:description: Sharyn Alfonsi, a CBS News journalist, said the expiration of her contract sent “a chilling message to the entire newsroom.”

item.title: The $400 Million Showdown Between a Billionaire and a California Mayor
item.description: The owner of the Los Angeles Rams and the City of Inglewood are in a dispute over Hollywood Park and SoFi Stadium, which is about to host World Cup matches.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens, Ken Belson and Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inglewood, Calif., argues that its 2015 development agreement with Hollywood Park, which includes SoFi Stadium, is void because of a subsequent state court decision.

item.title: Rubio’s India Visit Yields No Major Deals to Repair U.S.-India Relations
item.description: The secretary of state visited India to reassure the South Asian giant that it can still rely on the United States. India did not gain much from the visit.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/Agence France-Presse, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio and India’s foreign minister, Subrahmanyam Jaishankar, prepare to sign a memorandum of understanding in New Delhi on Tuesday.

item.title: Thornton Wilder’s Last Play Vanished Into Thin Air. Or Did It?
item.description: Decades after “The Emporium” failed to open on Broadway in 1954, one man went on a quest to find it.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.category: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library
item.media:credit: Fred Stein Archive/Archive Photos via Getty Images
item.media:description: Thornton Wilder won three Pulitzer Prizes, including one for the classic drama “Our Town,” still among the most produced plays in the United States.

item.title: The Viruses Causing New Outbreaks Are Much Less Familiar to Science
item.description: The types of Ebola and hantavirus worrying officials are very different from the species identified decades ago, raising new questions about how to respond.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: Uganda Closes Border With Congo as Ebola Fears Rise
item.description: Seven confirmed cases of the virus have already been reported in Kampala, the capital, but officials say the country has robust disease surveillance.
item.dc:creator: Musinguzi Blanshe and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:credit: Badru Katumba/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Red Cross workers moving the body of someone who may have died from Ebola, in Kampala, Uganda, on Tuesday.

item.title: Ferrari’s First Electric Car Runs Into Backlash in Italy and Beyond
item.description: Memes mocked the new model, analysts questioned its appeal and investors sold the automaker’s stock. A former Ferrari chairman warned of “the destruction of a legend.”
item.dc:creator: Bernhard Warner
item.media:credit: Ferrari Press Office, via AFP -- Getty Images
item.media:description: For help designing the fully electric car, Ferrari turned to the agency founded by Jony Ive, Apple’s former design chief, and the industrial designer Marc Newson.

item.title: Could Russia Hit Harder? It at Least Wants Ukraine to Think So.
item.description: Major attacks on Kyiv, followed by warnings of more, come as Moscow is stalled on the battlefield and at the negotiating table.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and Siobhán O’Grady
item.media:credit: Evgeniy Maloletka/Associated Press
item.media:description: A building burning after a Russian strike on Kyiv on Sunday.

item.title: High-Level British Spy Warns of Expanding Russia Threat
item.description: The director of Britain’s electronic surveillance agency warned that Moscow was getting more brazen, adding that estimates suggested 500,000 Russian soldiers had been killed in the Ukraine war.
item.dc:creator: Adam Goldman
item.media:credit: Igor Ivanko/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Vladimir Putin appearing on a screen during the Victory Day celebrations in Moscow earlier this month.

item.title: Knicks Fans Confront High Ticket Prices for NBA Finals Games at MSG
item.description: New York Knicks fans, still celebrating the team making it to the N.B.A. finals, are confronting high ticket prices for the games at Madison Square Garden.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans who grew up cheering for the New York Knicks said they couldn’t afford the high ticket prices listed in the presale.

item.title: Tiler Peck’s New Ballet Expands the Idea of What Female and Male Dancers Can Be
item.description: The idea of sisterhood and brotherhood flows through “Symphonie Espagnole,” Peck’s new work for New York City Ballet. We dissect two sections.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times

item.title: Writing Is Fundamental to How We Think
item.description: A.I. can be a crutch that hurts our ability to think creatively.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Winthrop

item.title: Time Gave Me Back What Prison Took Away
item.description: A formerly incarcerated writer reflects on how the prison system didn’t foster change, but befriending time did.
item.dc:creator: Marvin Wade and Evan Bode

item.title: War. Heat. Cigarettes.
item.description: Updates on stories from around the world.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: In Lebanon.

item.title: Ball State Settles With Ex-Employee for $225,000 Over Charlie Kirk Posts
item.description: Ball State University is the latest institution to agree to pay workers who lost their jobs over their posts about the conservative activist.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy W. Peters and Sabrina Tavernise
item.media:credit: Kaiti Sullivan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Suzanne Swierc was fired from her job as a health care administrator at Ball State University in Muncie, Ind.

item.title: After Decades on the Run, a Leftist Militant Is Sentenced in Germany
item.description: Daniela Klette, 67, was part of the Red Army Faction, which attacked American and capitalist interests until the 1990s. She was convicted of robberies committed later to finance life as a fugitive.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Sina Schuldt
item.media:description: Daniela Klette, a member of the Red Army Faction, in court in Verden, Germany, on Wednesday.

item.title: Where Time Is Always 15 Minutes Apart From Everywhere Else
item.description: In Nepal, a nation wedged between India and China, a unique time zone is just one expression of a singular national identity.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech and Hannah Reyes Morales
item.media:description: Ghanta Ghar, or the “Hour House,” during its renovation in Kathmandu. Nepal keeps its own time zone, 5 hours and 45 minutes ahead of Coordinated Universal Time.

item.title: Teachers Union Urges Schools to Curb A.I. Chatbots and Screen Time
item.description: The American Federation of Teachers recommended “no screens” at all for those in second grade or younger, and no A.I. chatbots for students in elementary school.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Singer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Randi Weingarten, president of the American Federation of Teachers, said on Wednesday that teaching and learning in the earliest grades “should be done without A.I.”
